heuristics.rule: Use L1-B as an H1B lottery bypass via overseas assignment, but plan the 5-year clock from day one
heuristics.condition: When an employer offers an L1-B transfer path requiring 1 year abroad and you have failed the H1B lottery multiple times...
heuristics.action: Accept the L1-B path but immediately start planning EB1B or EB2 green card filing within the 5-year L1-B window, not after it expires
heuristics.because: L1-B has a hard 5-year cap in the US; failure to convert within that window forces a return overseas for 1 year before a new L1 can be filed, resetting the clock

heuristics.rule: Cap-exempt H1B positions at research universities are a viable low-lottery-risk path to green card, then back to industry
heuristics.condition: When you have failed H1B lottery multiple times and are open to a temporary salary reduction...
heuristics.action: Target cap-exempt research university or nonprofit positions to obtain H1B status without lottery, pursue green card, then return to industry
heuristics.because: A commenter reported this path yielded green card in ~3 years with less visa anxiety, and the total timeline was comparable to waiting for H1B lottery luck

heuristics.rule: For EB1A self-petition, assess your credentials against all 10 USCIS criteria — meeting 3 is sufficient
heuristics.condition: When you are a non-Indian national with publications, open-source contributions, or documented professional influence...
heuristics.action: Evaluate EB1A self-petition seriously before defaulting to PERM-based EB2, since EB1A skips PERM (saving 2-4 years) and has minimal wait for non-Indian nationals
heuristics.because: Community commenter clarified EB1A criteria are broader than commonly assumed, and the PERM bypass is a substantial time advantage

decision_factors.factor: Nationality and EB green card priority date backlog
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Indian nationals face decade-plus waits for EB2/EB3; non-Indian nationals may have near-current priority dates, making EB1A or even EB2 far more viable
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the USCIS Visa Bulletin for your country of birth under the relevant EB category

decision_factors.factor: Employer's willingness and ability to support alternative visa paths (L1, EB1B, EB2)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Some employers offer L1 as a genuine bypass; others use it as a retention tool without intent to file green card; the difference determines whether this path resolves your status or extends dependency
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask explicitly: will the company file I-140 for EB1B or EB2 during the L1 period? Get a timeline commitment in writing if possible

decision_factors.factor: Tolerance for status dependency on a single employer
decision_factors.why_it_matters: All employer-sponsored paths (H1B, L1, PERM) tie you to one employer; loss of job means loss of status with limited grace periods
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess your financial runway, risk tolerance, and whether you would accept a worse job to preserve status

decision_factors.factor: Canada Express Entry CRS score eligibility
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A person with a strong tech background and Canadian work experience may be able to obtain Canadian PR in 1-2 years via CEC, which eliminates employer dependency entirely
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Use the IRCC CRS calculator with your current profile; compare expected ITA timeline to expected US green card timeline

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting L1-B transfer to Canada office
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The 5-year US L1-B maximum duration clock starts the moment you re-enter on L1; if green card is not filed and progressing well before year 4, you may be forced to leave the US again before status resolves
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: People who accept the L1 path without understanding the hard cap, especially those whose employers deprioritize green card filing after the L1 is approved

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting for H1B lottery year after year at a high-paying employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Each year of waiting is also a year during which Canadian Express Entry CRS score (and therefore invitation probability) may peak and then decline as younger candidates enter the pool; the optimal Canada window can close while you wait
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who treat H1B lottery as the only option and do not track their Canada eligibility in parallel

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking a cap-exempt research university role at lower salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Salary drop of ~30% is real, but the tradeoff includes no lottery risk, faster green card timeline, and lower psychological burden — total career value may be higher depending on the individual's risk pricing
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High earners at large tech companies who anchor their decision purely on current compensation without modeling the value of visa certainty

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating immigration lawyers as strategic advisors rather than execution agents
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: People assume that because lawyers are experts, they will proactively recommend the best path; in reality, most lawyers execute the path they are asked to execute
failure_patterns.consequence: Two people with identical profiles who ask different questions of the same lawyer end up on entirely different paths — one optimized, one default
warning_signs: You hired a lawyer but never asked 'what is my optimal path given my full profile?'
Your lawyer only ever discussed one visa category with you

failure_patterns.pattern: Waiting until after H1B lottery failure to start evaluating alternatives
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias — each year feels like this might be the lucky year; planning for failure feels like giving up
failure_patterns.consequence: Alternative paths (L1 setup, EB1A preparation, Canada CRS optimization) require months to years of lead time; starting after the third or fourth lottery loss means years of additional delay
warning_signs: You are applying for H1B lottery without a documented backup plan
You have not checked your Canada CRS score or EB1A eligibility despite multiple lottery failures

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your country of birth, and have you checked the current EB priority date for your category?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether PERM-based EB2/EB3 is viable on a reasonable timeline or effectively closed, which changes which alternative paths are worth pursuing
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If non-Indian and EB2 is current or near-current, EB1A self-petition or employer-sponsored EB2 with PERM is viable — compare to L1 timeline
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If Indian national, PERM-based paths are likely decade-long waits; focus evaluation on EB1A (no PERM required) or Canada PR as primary alternatives

diagnostic_questions.question: Has your employer explicitly committed to filing I-140 during your L1 period, and by what date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Distinguishes employers using L1 as a genuine green card bridge from those using it as a retention mechanism with no green card intent
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Validate that the I-140 category and timeline fits within your L1-B 5-year window with margin
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Treat the L1 as a visa-only solution, not a green card path; evaluate independent routes (EB1A self-petition, Canada PR) in parallel

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have publications, open-source contributions, speaking invitations, or documented peer recognition in your field?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether EB1A self-petition is worth a serious evaluation — three of the ten USCIS criteria met is sufficient
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Consult an EB1A-specialized attorney for a credential audit; the PERM bypass alone could save 2-4 years
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Focus on building qualifying criteria proactively (peer review, open source, media citations) if EB1A is a medium-term goal

industry_realities.common_belief: EB1A (extraordinary ability) is only for Nobel Prize winners or top academics — it is irrelevant for software engineers
industry_realities.actual_reality: EB1A requires meeting 3 of 10 USCIS criteria; tech professionals with peer-reviewed publications, significant open-source contributions, or documented industry influence have successfully self-petitioned; the bar is high but not academic-elite-only
industry_realities.implication: SWEs who dismiss EB1A without a credential audit may be forgoing the fastest available green card path (no PERM, no employer dependency, minimal wait for non-Indian nationals)

industry_realities.common_belief: Cap-exempt H1B jobs at universities or nonprofits are a niche workaround not worth pursuing due to lower pay
industry_realities.actual_reality: Multiple community members confirmed this path results in real H1B status without lottery risk, and the green card timeline can be shorter than waiting for H1B lottery luck plus PERM; some returned to industry afterward with green card in hand
industry_realities.implication: The salary discount should be modeled as a time-bounded cost, not a permanent penalty; for people who have failed lottery 3+ times, the expected value calculation often favors the cap-exempt path

industry_realities.common_belief: The L1-B company transfer to Canada is a trap or unusual workaround
industry_realities.actual_reality: Large tech companies routinely use L1-B as a structured H1B lottery bypass; the path is well-established — 1 year in Canada office → L1-B petition → return to US; the risk is the 5-year hard cap, not the path itself
industry_realities.implication: Candidates should evaluate the L1-B offer on its merits (green card filing commitment, 5-year planning) rather than rejecting it out of unfamiliarity

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How to use an immigration lawyer
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Hire a lawyer and assume they will recommend the optimal path; defer entirely to whatever they suggest
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Research the landscape of available paths independently, identify which options fit your profile, then direct the lawyer to evaluate and execute the chosen strategy
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Lawyers are compliance executors, not strategic optimizers; the person who asks 'what is my optimal path' gets a different (and better) outcome than the person who asks 'help me file X'

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Apply for the lottery each year and wait for results before thinking about alternatives
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintain a parallel track: assess EB1A eligibility, Canada CRS score, and L1 eligibility at all times; treat lottery as one bet among several, not the primary plan
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Alternative paths require months to years of lead time; reactive planning after lottery failure wastes the preparation window

community_misconceptions.misconception: L1-B is a risky or unusual path that big companies only offer as a trap to get you to move abroad
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The requirement to work abroad for a year feels punitive; employees interpret it as the company offloading risk onto them
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: L1-B intracompany transfer for overseas employees is a well-established, legal H1B lottery bypass used routinely by large tech companies; the genuine risk is the 5-year US duration cap, not the path structure
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates who reject L1-B based on this misconception may continue losing H1B lotteries indefinitely while a viable path was available

community_misconceptions.misconception: Once a lawyer is handling your immigration, you don't need to understand the strategy — that's what you're paying them for
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Professional services framing — you pay an expert so you don't have to think about it; this works for most professional services but not immigration strategy
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Immigration lawyers are responsible for document compliance and legal filings, not for identifying your optimal multi-path strategy; strategic decisions must be made by the applicant
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants who abdicate strategy to their lawyer may be on a default path (e.g., PERM EB2) when a faster path (EB1A self-petition) was available and never mentioned

real_case_outcomes.situation: SDE at a major tech company (L4, $170K base), failed H1B lottery 3 consecutive years, employer offered L1-B via Canada office
real_case_outcomes.decision: The community provided the outcome data: one commenter took the cap-exempt university researcher path instead of L1-B
real_case_outcomes.outcome: Green card obtained in approximately 3 years via cap-exempt H1B + university employer sponsorship; subsequently returned to industry with green card in hand; total timeline comparable to L1 path but with lower anxiety and no employer lock-in during the critical period
real_case_outcomes.lesson: For repeated H1B lottery failures, cap-exempt university positions can be a faster and lower-stress green card path than waiting for lottery luck, even with a significant salary reduction

real_case_outcomes.situation: Chinese national in similar H1B lottery failure situation, chose to pursue Canadian PR via Express Entry CEC
real_case_outcomes.decision: Transitioned to Canada, accumulated qualifying work experience, obtained PR through Canadian Experience Class
real_case_outcomes.outcome: After obtaining Canadian PR, salary negotiation leverage improved significantly because the candidate was no longer employer-dependent for immigration status; described as removing 'identity anxiety' and improving career decision clarity
real_case_outcomes.lesson: Obtaining PR in Canada (or any jurisdiction) removes employer-dependency and changes negotiating dynamics; the career value of status independence should be weighted against the salary or opportunity cost of the transition

psychological_barriers.trigger: Asking whether L1-B is a 'trap' after multiple H1B lottery failures
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Sunk cost framing combined with loss aversion — each lottery attempt represents an investment in the US path; evaluating alternatives feels like admitting the investment was wasted
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: User frames every alternative path through the lens of 'is this a trap?' rather than 'what is the expected value of each path given my goals?'
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Reframe from 'trap vs. not trap' to 'what does year 10 look like under each scenario?' — this forces expected value thinking and reduces the emotional weight of the sunk cost

psychological_barriers.trigger: Describing Canada PR or returning home as 'giving up' or 'failure' compared to getting a US green card
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Identity anchoring — the US green card has become a proxy for professional success or personal worth, rather than one of several tools for building a good career
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Users accept worse jobs, more employer dependency, or longer timelines specifically to preserve the US path, without modeling whether the US outcome actually serves their 10-year goals better
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Directly challenge the framing: 'The question is not whether you got a US green card — the question is where you are in 10 years. Those are not the same question.'

reframing_moves.original_framing: I need to figure out whether the L1-B path my company is offering is a trap or not
reframing_moves.reframe: The question is not whether L1-B is a trap — it is whether your employer has committed to filing your green card during the L1 window, and whether that timeline fits within the 5-year hard cap
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Shifts the question from a binary trust judgment (trap vs. not trap) to a concrete, verifiable set of conditions the person can actually evaluate and negotiate
reframing_moves.example_language: L1-B本身不是坑，坑在于你的公司有没有承诺在L1期间帮你办绿卡，以及时间线是否在5年上限内

reframing_moves.original_framing: I failed the H1B lottery three times — what are my options?
reframing_moves.reframe: Stop treating H1B as the primary plan with alternatives as fallbacks. You have three parallel tracks (L1-B, EB1A self-petition, Canada PR) that require lead time — the question is which combination to run simultaneously starting now
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Converts a reactive crisis mindset ('what do I do now that I failed') into a proactive portfolio mindset ('which tracks am I running in parallel'), which is how experienced candidates actually navigate this system
reframing_moves.example_language: 真正的问题不是抽不中怎么办，而是你现在是否已经在同时推进多条路径

reframing_moves.original_framing: Not getting a US green card feels like failure compared to my peers
reframing_moves.reframe: The real question is: 10 years from now, where will your comprehensive development be better? A US green card is one path among several — not the destination itself
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Breaks the identity anchoring on the green card as a success metric by substituting a longer time horizon and a broader outcome measure (career, life quality, autonomy)
reframing_moves.example_language: 真正的问题是：10年后你在哪里的综合发展更好？美国绿卡只是众多路径之一，不是终点
